data.title: Adam Back Denies NYT Claim Naming Him Bitcoin Creator Satoshi Nakamoto
data.description:
Blockstream CEO Adam Back has firmly rejected claims by The New York Times that he is the mysterious creator of Bitcoin, Satoshi Nakamoto. The investigation, led by Pulitzer Prize winner John Carreyrou, reportedly analyzed 620 suspects over 18 months, using stylistic forensics, technical expertise, and historical communications to make the connection.

Forensic linguistics points to Back

Carreyrou’s investigation relied on a detailed stylistic and technical analysis, comparing years of forum posts, emails, and coding communication. The reporter noted that Back’s idiosyncrasies—including British spellings, double-spacing habits, and repeated technical phrasing—closely mirrored patterns in Satoshi’s writings.

Here’s the first half of the video put together by John Carreyrou from The New York Times, arguing that Adam Back is Satoshi.

You can watch the rest from here:https://t.co/VMWFgmZ6gVpic.twitter.com/BU7p8qFGfj

— Ismailoğlu Pasha (@IsmailogluF) April 8, 2026

Back’s early work on Hashcash, the proof-of-work system foundational to Bitcoin, along with his early concern about email spam, further fueled Carreyrou’s hypothesis. His deep expertise in public-key cryptography also aligned with the skills necessary to develop Bitcoin’s security architecture, strengthening the stylistic case.

Back took to X to firmly deny the report, writing,

“I am not Satoshi. The article is a combination of coincidence and similar phrases from people with similar experience and interests.”

i'm not satoshi, but I was early in laser focus on the positive societal implications of cryptography, online privacy and electronic cash, hence my ~1992 onwards active interest in applied research on ecash, privacy tech on cypherpunks list which led to hashcash and other ideas.

— Adam Back (@adam3us) April 8, 2026

Carreyrou pointed to an exchange in El Salvador where he claimed that Back's slip of the tongue has confirmed his suspicion that he (Back) was indeed Satoshi.

Carreyrou pointed out the uncanny similarities between Satoshi's statement that "he was better with codes than words" with Back statement that said "I did a lot of talking for somebody, I mean... I mean, I'm not saying I'm good with words, but I sure did a lot of yakking on these lists actually."

Back later clarified that the remark merely reflected his verbosity on the Bitcoin development mailing list, not a personal revelation of identity.

The report sparked immediate discussion across crypto circles. Bitwise president Teddy Fusaro quipped, “Satoshi is doing a bitcoin treasury company with Cantor Fitzgerald. That definitely passes the smell test,” highlighting skepticism about linking Back’s current public role to the pseudonymous creator.

Yes, I'm sure Satoshi is doing a bitcoin treasury company with Cantor Fitzgerald.

That definitely passes the smell test.

— Teddy Fusaro (@teddyfuse) April 8, 2026

Legacy of speculation: McAfee and the ongoing hunt for Satoshi

Back is not the first figure linked to Satoshi through stylistic analysis. Before his death, John McAfee independently suggested Back as a candidate, noting similar writing habits in the Bitcoin whitepaper. Over the years, countless figures—including Hal Finney, Nick Szabo, and Dorian Nakamoto—have been cited as potential creators.

2010: Hal Finney is Satoshi

2012: Nick Szabo is Satoshi

2014: Dorian Nakamoto is Satoshi

2016: Craig Wright is Satoshi

2018: Adam Back is Satoshi

2020: Jack Dorsey is Satoshi

2022: Elon Musk is Satoshi

2024: Peter Todd is Satoshi

2026: Epstein is Satoshi#Bitcoin… https://t.co/d0AOKYVuGy

— Li₿an_₿itcoin⚡️ (@Liban_Bitcoin) February 7, 2026

Despite the New York Times exposé, Adam Back remains unverified as Bitcoin’s creator. The case illustrates the enduring mystique of Satoshi Nakamoto, whose anonymity has become a defining feature of Bitcoin itself—protecting both the protocol and the mythos of the trillion-dollar network.

data.title: Anthropic Builds Powerful AI For Cyber Defence That Finds Critical Flaws But Keeps It Out Of Public Hands
data.description:
AI Model Reveals Decades-Old Software Flaws Faster Than Humans Can Detect

Anthropic has unveiled Claude Mythos Preview, a new artificial intelligence model capable of finding software vulnerabilities with a precision and speed that surpasses nearly all human experts.

The model is currently restricted to a select group of cybersecurity and technology partners, and the public will not have access.

Introducing Project Glasswing: an urgent initiative to help secure the world’s most critical software.

It’s powered by our newest frontier model, Claude Mythos Preview, which can find software vulnerabilities better than all but the most skilled humans.https://t.co/NQ7IfEtYk7

— Anthropic (@AnthropicAI) April 7, 2026

Which Organisations Are Using Mythos Preview

The launch consortium includes major technology companies such as Amazon Web Services, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks.

In addition, more than 40 organisations responsible for critical software infrastructure have been given access.

Anthropic has pledged up to $100 million in usage credits and $4 million in support for open-source security groups.

Thousands of Vulnerabilities Found Across Major Systems

Anthropic claims Mythos Preview has identified thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across every major operating system and web browser.

Among these are a 27-year-old flaw in OpenBSD that could allow an attacker to remotely crash a machine simply by connecting to it, and a 16-year-old vulnerability in FFmpeg, which automated tools had scanned over five million times without detecting the issue.

An AI just found a bug humans missed for 27 years.

Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview scanned major OS and browser, found 1000s of hidden vulnerabilities in hours.

This is what AI-powered cybersecurity looks like.#ProjectGlasswing#Anthropic#ClaudeMythos#CynicalTechnologypic.twitter.com/PYXmJwPuP5

— Cynical Technology (@cynical_sec) April 9, 2026

The model also demonstrated the ability to chain multiple flaws in the Linux kernel, potentially allowing an attacker to escalate from ordinary user access to full system control.

The company plans to publish cryptographic hashes for some of these bugs now, with more detailed disclosures to follow after patches are deployed.

Anthropic highlighted that Mythos Preview independently discovered most vulnerabilities and even developed related exploits.

How Mythos Preview Performs Compared With Previous Models

On CyberGym, Mythos Preview achieved an 83.1% success rate in vulnerability reproduction, compared with 66.6% for Claude Opus 4.6.

In the SWE-bench Verified test, Mythos scored 93.9%, surpassing Opus 4.6’s 80.8% performance.

🚨Holy...Claude Mythos is crazy
SWE-bench Verified:
- Mythos: 93.9%
- Opus 4.6: 80.8%
How much has it increased? 14%? 17%?
No, it's 3.3 TIMES!!

SWE-bench Multimodal 59.0% vs 27.1% Doubled
CyberGym Vulnerability Reproduction 83.1% vs 66.6%
GPQA Diamond: 94.6%

Too… pic.twitter.com/N1IlTwwku2

— Team9 (@Team9_ai) April 8, 2026

Anthropic stated that AI models have now reached a level of coding capability where they can exceed nearly all human experts in finding and exploiting software weaknesses.

Project Glasswing Arms Defenders Before Attackers Strike

Under Project Glasswing, participating organisations will use Mythos Preview for defensive cybersecurity operations, including local vulnerability detection, black-box testing of binaries, endpoint security, and penetration testing.

CrowdStrike’s chief technology officer, Elia Zaitsev, said,

“The window between a vulnerability being discovered and being exploited by an adversary has collapsed – what once took months now happens in minutes with AI.”

Anthropic has also committed $2.5 million to Alpha-Omega and OpenSSF through the Linux Foundation, and $1.5 million to the Apache Software Foundation to strengthen open-source security.

Early testing has shown the model can help organisations detect and fix software and hardware vulnerabilities at a scale and speed previously unattainable.

Industry Leaders React to the AI Threat

AWS reported it analyses more than 400 trillion network flows a day, while Microsoft said Mythos Preview demonstrated gains on CTI-REALM.

Google confirmed it will make the model accessible via Vertex AI, and Palo Alto Networks emphasised the need for defensive tools before attackers exploit these capabilities.

Cisco’s chief security officer, Anthony Grieco, commented:

“AI capabilities have crossed a threshold that fundamentally changes the urgency required to protect critical infrastructure from cyber threats, and there is no going back.”

AI Used by Attackers Raises Urgency

The New York Times reported that late last year state-backed Chinese hackers exploited Anthropic AI to target roughly 30 companies and government agencies, with human operators handling just 10% to 20% of the work.

AI is already being used to draft phishing emails, write ransom notes, sort stolen data, and accelerate breach sales.

anthropic just pulled the fire alarm on the first large scale ai orchestrated espionage campaign and then calmly walked everyone through how they punched it in the face. a chinese state backed crew jailbroke claude code into doing 80 to 90 percent of the dirty work itself… pic.twitter.com/XGkfqp0BWX

— 🍓🍓🍓 (@iruletheworldmo) November 13, 2025

Anthropic emphasised that without proper safeguards, these capabilities could enable adversaries to exploit flaws in critical software at unprecedented speed.

Claude Mythos Demonstrates Power and Risk

Mike Krieger of Anthropic explained at a HumanX AI conference in San Francisco:

“We have a new model that we’re explicitly not releasing to the public.”

Instead, the company is equipping cybersecurity experts and open-source engineers to use Mythos as a defensive tool.

The oldest vulnerabilities identified by the AI date back 27 years, with many having gone unnoticed despite extensive human review and automated testing.

Anthropic said it has also been in discussions with the U.S. government about Mythos, despite a federal court temporarily halting a White House directive to terminate Anthropic contracts.

The company frames Project Glasswing as the starting point in defending global digital infrastructure, stressing that no single organisation can manage cybersecurity threats alone and that urgent action is needed.

AI Threats Accelerate Cybersecurity Arms Race

The rapid rise of generative AI has drastically reduced the skill, cost, and time required to find critical software bugs.

Anthropic warned that without proactive safeguards, AI could make cyberattacks far more frequent and damaging, posing risks to economies, public safety, and national security.

data.title: DeFi Security FAQ: Industry Security Lessons from the Drift Asset Theft Incident
data.description:
Shaw.AI, Jinse Finance

On April 2nd, Beijing time, Drift Protocol, a leading derivatives trading platform in the Solana ecosystem, experienced a major security incident, with confirmed on-chain data losses exceeding $285 million. This is one of the largest DeFi security incidents in the ecosystem since the Wormhole bridge attack in 2022. Based on this incident and common security questions in the industry, we have compiled the following FAQs to help clarify the core pain points, attack logic, and protection methods of DeFi security.

Q1: What was the core reason for the Drift Protocol theft? Was it not a vulnerability in the contract code?

The core reason for this theft was not a vulnerability in the smart contract code, but a composite attack of social engineering, privilege hijacking, and abuse of technical tools. The essence is a "human failure" rather than a "code failure."

Specifically, the attackers used social engineering to induce two members of the Drift security committee to pre-sign malicious transactions. They then exploited Solana's durable nonce feature to trigger the transactions, seizing administrator privileges. Subsequently, they tampered with the oracle, bypassed withdrawal risk controls, and used fake collateral to obtain real assets. The entire attack was completed in just 12 minutes. It's worth noting that Drift's newly migrated 2/5 multi-signature mechanism lacked a time lock, allowing for instantaneous privilege transfer, making it one of the key vulnerabilities that enabled the successful attack.

Q2: What assets were involved in this theft? Why did the loss reach $285 million?

Q2:

This attack involved multiple core Drift pools, including JLP Delta Neutral, SOL Super Staking, and BTC Super Staking. The stolen assets encompassed various crypto assets: 41.7 million JLP tokens worth approximately $155 million, USDC approximately $60.4 million, cbBTC approximately $11.3 million, wBTC approximately $4.4 million, and SOL and other assets approximately $54.3 million, totaling over $285 million in losses. The core reason for the massive scale of the losses is that after gaining administrator privileges, the attackers raised the withdrawal thresholds for five markets, including USDC/SOL, from the $500 million level to the $500 trillion level, completely shutting down risk control, and emptying nearly 20 vaults in batches. Furthermore, the attack was meticulously planned (taking three weeks) and went undetected in time. Q3: Where did the stolen funds ultimately go? Were any funds recovered? The flow of the stolen funds is clear and concealed. The overall path is: Drift Protocol vault → exchanged for stablecoins on the Jupiter platform → transferred to the Ethereum network via cross-chain bridges such as Wormhole and CCTP → some funds were transferred to mixing tools such as Tornado Cash to conceal the funds' trajectory. It is currently known that Circle has frozen approximately $4 million worth of stolen USDC, and the remaining funds have not yet been recovered. On-chain characteristics indicate that the attack is suspected to be directed at the North Korean Lazarus Group (preliminary assessment by multiple security companies). Q4: What countermeasures have the Drift Protocol project team and the Solana ecosystem taken? Following the incident, the Drift project team immediately took emergency measures: suspending protocol deposit and withdrawal functions, establishing a special investigation team to investigate the details of the attack, promising compensation to affected users, and announcing a restructuring of the multi-signature mechanism and permission management system. The Solana Foundation has also explicitly stated that this incident was caused by a social engineering attack and operational errors, not by vulnerabilities in the Solana chain itself or its protocol code. Meanwhile, the Solana ecosystem has begun promoting industry standards, requiring relevant projects to strengthen multi-signature security and restrict the use of durable nonces.

Q5: What are the differences between this incident and the $326 million Wormhole bridge attack in 2022?

While both are significant DeFi attacks within the Solana ecosystem, their core differences are significant: The Wormhole attack was a **smart contract code vulnerability attack**, where attackers exploited flaws in the contract signature verification logic to forge deposit certificates and mint fake wETH, ultimately stealing $326 million. The root cause was a code design flaw. In contrast, the Drift attack was a **permission and operational security vulnerability attack**, primarily involving social engineering forged signatures, the lack of time locks for multi-signatures, and abuse of permissions. The root cause was negligence at the governance and operational levels. Both incidents reflect weaknesses in the Solana ecosystem's DeFi security governance.

Q6: What is a multi-signature mechanism? Why do security issues arise with multi-signature?

Multi-signature (multi-signature) is a commonly used security mechanism in DeFi projects. It refers to the requirement of authorization from two or more signers to complete critical operations such as asset transfer and permission modification. It is often used for the management of project treasuries and fund pools, with the initial purpose of diversifying risks and avoiding single points of failure.

Multi-signature is a security mechanism commonly used in DeFi projects. It refers to the requirement of authorization from two or more signers to complete critical operations such as asset transfer and permission modification. It is often used for the management of project treasuries and fund pools. The initial purpose is to diversify risks and avoid single points of failure.

Q8: Is oracle manipulation a common DeFi attack method? How to prevent it?

Yes, oracles, as the "data heart" of DeFi, are responsible for transmitting off-chain price data to on-chain, making them a key target for attackers. This Drift attack involved oracle manipulation. The attackers deployed fake tokens (CVT), creating false prices through volume manipulation to deceive the oracle, and then used the fake tokens as collateral to obtain real assets within the protocol. The core vulnerability of this type of attack is the oracle's single data source or weak verification mechanism.

Q8: Is oracle manipulation a common DeFi attack method? How to prevent it?

Q8: Yes, oracles, as the "data heart" of DeFi, are responsible for transmitting off-chain price data to on-chain, making them a key target for attackers.

Prevention methods mainly include: using multi-source oracles for cross-validation (such as combining Chainlink, Band Protocol, etc.), setting up price anomaly circuit breakers (such as automatically suspending the function when price fluctuations exceed 20%), and strictly verifying the authenticity and liquidity of collateral to prevent a single fake asset from becoming "legitimate collateral." Q9: How can ordinary users judge the security of DeFi projects and avoid pitfalls? In light of the recent Drift incident, ordinary users can assess the security of DeFi projects and mitigate security risks from five dimensions: **Check Permissions and Multisignature Settings:** Confirm whether the project uses a multisignature mechanism, whether a time lock is set (to prevent instant permission transfer), and whether the number of multisignature signers and the review process are reasonable; **Verify Contract Audit Reports:** Prioritize projects audited by authoritative institutions such as CertiK and SlowMist, and whose audit reports show no major vulnerabilities. Avoid using unaudited or questionably audited projects; **Pay Attention to Oracles and Risk Control:** Understand whether the project's oracle data sources are diverse and whether there are robust risk control mechanisms (such as withdrawal limits and collateral verification); **Be Wary of High-Risk Features:** Be wary of features using durable... Be cautious of projects with features like nonce and unlimited authorization, and understand their security measures; Diversify your investments and control risks: Do not deposit all your assets into a single DeFi protocol, avoid excessively pursuing high returns while neglecting security, and pay attention to official project updates and respond promptly to security alerts. Q10: Besides the type of attack this time, what other common security risks exist in DeFi? The security risks in the DeFi industry are diverse. Besides social engineering and privilege escalation, the following are some common risks: Contract code vulnerabilities: such as reentrancy attacks, flash loan attacks, and logic vulnerabilities. 90% of developers have encountered these issues. In 2022, Mango Markets lost $110 million due to a flash loan attack. Phishing attacks: Attackers use fake official websites, fake advertisements (such as Google search ads), and phishing links to trick users into connecting wallets and authorizing malicious contracts to steal assets. In 2025, Aave suffered a large-scale Google ad phishing attack. Private key and authorization risks: Leaked user private keys, unlimited wallet authorization, or the theft of a multi-signature signer's private key can all lead to asset theft. Project scams: Some DeFi projects lack real-world application scenarios, relying solely on marketing to attract funds, ultimately absconding with the money and causing user asset losses. Q11: If a DeFi project experiences a security incident, how can users minimize their losses? If you encounter a security incident such as a DeFi project being hacked, you can take three steps to minimize losses: Immediately stop losses: Stop depositing any funds into the project, revoke all authorizations to the project from your wallet, and prevent further asset loss; Preserve evidence: Keep your transaction records, wallet address, asset flow statements, etc., pay attention to the compensation announcements issued by the project team, and submit proof of loss as required; Follow official updates: Follow the project team's investigation progress and compensation plan, and contact security agencies (such as PeckShield and SlowMist Technology) to provide relevant information and assist in tracking stolen funds. Q12: What long-term impact will this Drift incident have on the DeFi industry? This incident will drive the DeFi industry to strengthen security governance, especially the Solana ecosystem. The main impacts are concentrated in four areas: First, the standardization of multi-signature mechanisms, mandating time locks and improved signature verification processes to prevent abuse of permissions; second, the standardization of the use of features such as durable nonces, implementing a whitelist system to restrict their use cases; third, the strengthening of oracle security, with multi-source verification and price circuit breakers becoming industry standards; and fourth, increased industry awareness of social engineering attacks, with project teams strengthening security training for core members and improving identity verification mechanisms. At the same time, this incident will also make users and project teams more rational, no longer blindly believing that "multi-signature = security," and integrating security awareness into every aspect of project governance and user operations. In conclusion, the core appeal of DeFi lies in decentralization, high liquidity, and high returns, but security remains its lifeline. The $285 million theft from Drift Protocol serves as a stark reminder that DeFi security relies not only on rigorous code but also on sound governance mechanisms, standardized operational processes, and robust human security management. For project teams, it's crucial to abandon superficial security measures and build a strong three-tiered defense system encompassing access control, risk verification, and auditing. For ordinary users, it's essential to avoid complacency, acquire basic security knowledge, and carefully select projects to safeguard their assets amidst the DeFi wave.

data.title: The New York Times reignites the "Satoshi Nakamoto" speculation: Is Mr. Buck the answer?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 8, 2026, The New York Times published an article stating that John Carreyrou spent a year sifting through thousands of decades-old internet posts in search of the creator of Bitcoin. Ultimately, he concluded that the creator of Bitcoin had been hiding his identity as "Satoshi Nakamoto" for 17 years, and that a 55-year-old computer scientist named Adam Barker was Satoshi Nakamoto.

As information about this "new Satoshi Nakamoto" continued to circulate, Michael Saylor refuted The New York Times' investigation into Satoshi Nakamoto's identity. He stated that unless someone could sign using Satoshi Nakamoto's private key, all related speculations were merely theories.

As information about Barker, this "new Satoshi Nakamoto," continued to circulate, Michael Saylor refuted The New York Times' investigation into Satoshi Nakamoto's identity. He stated that unless someone could sign using Satoshi Nakamoto's private key, all related speculations were just that—speculations.

John Carreyrou also mentioned in his article: "Every failure has gloated many members of the Bitcoin community. As they like to point out, only Satoshi Nakamoto could ultimately prove his identity by transferring some Bitcoin. Any evidence below this standard can only be indirect." Is Buck Satoshi Nakamoto? What are the latest pieces of evidence? Who else has been considered Satoshi Nakamoto?

(Adam Buck)

I. John Carreyrou's Deduction

1. Origin of the Deduction

John Carreyrou has always believed that Satoshi Nakamoto's true identity is one of the great mysteries of our time, and has tried to find it many times, but all to no avail. Two years ago, he even spent several months researching this problem and wrote a book about it. But he soon realized that he knew very little about it and had to give up helplessly. He found the conclusions of *Money Electric: The Bitcoin Mystery* (https://www.hbomax.com/movies/money-electric-the-bitcoin-mystery/90e45730-bcb5-4525-aaea-44425a77c531) unconvincing: HBO concluded that a Canadian software developer was Satoshi Nakamoto based on only some seemingly very weak evidence. However, one scene caught his attention while watching this otherwise entertaining exploration of the cryptocurrency world. British cryptographer and Bitcoin activist Adam Barker sat on a park bench in Riga, Latvia, his brown jacket draped over his shirt. The filmmaker casually rattled off the names of several suspected Satoshi Nakamotos. When asked for his own name, Mr. Barker immediately tensed up, vehemently denying that he was Satoshi Nakamoto and demanding that the conversation remain confidential. John Carreyrou found Mr. Buck's behavior—his shifty eyes, awkward laughter, and unnatural movements with his left hand—very suspicious. He watched the segment repeatedly on television after the end credits. Mr. Buck's reaction reminded him of something else. An Australian imposter had been prosecuted for falsely claiming to be Satoshi Nakamoto. What if the evidence revealed in that case, heard in London a few months earlier, could help him unravel the mystery? 2. Clues He narrowed down his search: from small details like vocabulary usage to larger aspects like Buck and Nakamoto's technological philosophies, John Carreyrou compared them all. 1) Similar Vocabulary Usage One striking feature in Nakamoto's emails and other writings to Mr. Malmy is his mix of British spelling and idioms with American expressions. He carefully studied Satoshi Nakamoto's writings, especially the emails published by Mr. Malmy, and listed the words and phrases that impressed him. He used the advanced search function of the social media platform X to conduct an initial search to see if any of the dozen or so people most frequently suspected of being Satoshi Nakamoto had used the keywords he had marked—one person matched almost all the words and phrases he searched for perfectly: Mr. Buck. Furthermore, Mr. Buck invented Hashcash, a statistical puzzle system that Satoshi Nakamoto used for Bitcoin mining. Satoshi Nakamoto also cited Mr. Buck and Hashcash in his white paper. Additionally, there is some speculation that Satoshi Nakamoto is from Japan. Coincidentally, Mr. Buck showed interest in Japan as early as 1997. 2) Similarities between Buck and Satoshi Nakamoto Other similarities exist between Mr. Buck and Satoshi Nakamoto: Both dislike copyright. Mr. Buck open-sourced his Hashcash spam-limiting software; Satoshi Nakamoto did something similar. He released Bitcoin software under the MIT Open Source License, allowing anyone to use, modify, and distribute it without restriction. Both created dedicated internet mailing lists for their work—the Hashcash list and the Bitcoin-dev list. Both considered Napster's demise a cautionary tale. Back in May 2000, Mr. Buck made the exact same comparison between Napster and Gnutella: Napster's servers were centralized and could be shut down, while Gnutella was essentially impossible to shut down.

3) Ten Years Before the Birth of BTC: Buck's Vision

On April 30, 1997, Buck proposed creating an electronic cash system "completely decoupled" from the modern banking system. This system should possess four key characteristics: protecting the privacy of payers and payees; distributed across a computer network, making it difficult to shut down; having a built-in scarcity to prevent excessive inflation; and requiring no trust in any individual or bank. To achieve the last point, two days later he proposed a fifth component: a publicly verifiable protocol.

These five elements later became the core of Bitcoin.

Four months later, Mr. Buck revisited the topic of electronic cash, introducing new features based on game theory.

He wrote: “I have seriously considered an application scenario: creating a distributed banking system. Ideally, in this system, all nodes are equivalent, and only when k (or n) of these nodes collude can the bank be disrupted.” Mr. Buck was referring to the Byzantine Generals Problem, a computer science puzzle that plagues distributed systems. In this analogy, n generals besiege an enemy city at war with Byzantium. To successfully invade, they must attack simultaneously, but k of the generals might be traitors who could sabotage the plan. Distributed computer networks face the same problem: malicious actors (or nodes) within the network can disrupt it. Mr. Buck wanted to create an electronic cash network with so many nodes distributed in so many different places that anyone wanting to disrupt it would not find enough accomplices. This sounds very similar to the system Satoshi Nakamoto described in his white paper published 11 years later: Nakamoto wrote that Bitcoin could work “as long as the majority of CPU power is controlled by nodes that are not cooperating in attacking the network.” In a 1997 post on the Cypherpunks mailing list, Mr. Barker wrote that some nodes could “come and go freely” without affecting the operation of the network. Satoshi Nakamoto, in his white paper, wrote that nodes could “leave and rejoin the network at will.” The wording is slightly different, but even those who are not top cryptographers can see that Mr. Barker and Satoshi Nakamoto proposed the exact same concept. … In a December 1998 comment on “b-money” (a cryptocurrency), Mr. Barker foresaw Satoshi Nakamoto’s solution to inflation. Any electronic currency minted by solving computational problems will inevitably suffer from hyperinflation because as computer chip performance improves, it becomes easier for computers to solve these problems and mint new currencies. To solve this problem, Mr. Barker suggested that minting a “b-money” currency should “require more computational work over time.” This is exactly how Satoshi Nakamoto designed the Bitcoin software. His program made it take an average of 10 minutes to mine each new Bitcoin transaction block, and he created an algorithm that would increase the difficulty of solving the problem as faster computer chips began to shorten this time interval. As if these visionary insights weren't enough, Mr. Barker proposed another crucial concept in April 1999. For a distributed electronic cash system to function effectively, each transaction needs a public and immutable timestamp. Otherwise, users could spend the same coin twice, throwing the entire system into chaos. Mr. Barker's solution was to use hash trees—condensing large amounts of data into a digital fingerprint—and publish these fingerprints in New York Times classified ads. Satoshi Nakamoto adopted the same idea in Bitcoin, but he replaced the classified ad component with Mr. Barker's HashCash, which adds timestamps to transactions by making the intensive computation required to package transactions into blocks too expensive and time-consuming. Mr. Barker even foreshadowed Satoshi Nakamoto's response to one of the major criticisms Bitcoin later faced—its high power consumption. He argued in 1998 and 1999 that the combined energy consumption of Hashcash and b-money might be less than that of the banking system. Ten years later, when an early reader of the Bitcoin white paper raised this question, Satoshi Nakamoto made a similar argument. In short, a decade before Bitcoin's inception, Mr. Barker foresaw almost every aspect of Bitcoin and used the same reasoning as Satoshi Nakamoto to excuse its major flaws. … In the fall of 2014, Mr. Barker and his Blockstream colleagues published a white paper introducing an innovation he conceived called "anchored sidechains." This paper, with Mr. Barker as the first author, mentioned DigiCash. Founded by cryptographer David Chaum in the late 1980s, DigiCash created a groundbreaking electronic currency. Unlike Bitcoin, DigiCash relied on a central server it owned and operated. When DigiCash went bankrupt in 1998, the currency also disappeared. The paper's introduction states, "The need for a central server became DigiCash's Achilles' heel." This is precisely Satoshi Nakamoto's description of DigiCash's flaw five years ago: "Of course, the biggest difference is the lack of a central server. This is the Achilles' heel of the Chaumian system." II. Who else has been considered Satoshi Nakamoto? 1. Nick Szabo A Hungarian-American computer scientist who proposed a Bitcoin-like concept in 1998 called "BitGold" and is considered a pioneer of Bitcoin. He is also known for using pseudonyms since the 1990s. However, he has repeatedly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto publicly, and there is no evidence of direct involvement in the Bitcoin code, nor is there any clear record of communication with Satoshi Nakamoto. In a May 2011 article, Szabo, discussing the creator of Bitcoin, stated: "Of all the people I know, only myself, David, and Hal Finney were interested enough in this idea to actually implement it. Then Nakamoto appeared (assuming Nakamoto is neither Finney nor David)." 2. Hal Finney Hal Finney was a cryptographer and a member of Cypherpunk. In 2004, he developed RPOW (Proof-of-Work); in 2009, he received the first Bitcoin transaction; he died in 2014 from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Finney is considered Satoshi Nakamoto because he was one of the earliest people to directly collaborate with Nakamoto, and their technical abilities were perfectly matched (cryptography + P2P systems), and their writing styles were highly similar. Furthermore, Satoshi Nakamoto disappeared between 2010 and 2011, coinciding with Finney's withdrawal from development due to illness. However, he publicly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto, stating he was merely "someone who communicated with Satoshi Nakamoto," more of a collaborator than an anonymous contributor. In April 2009, while Satoshi Nakamoto was sending emails and Bitcoins to others, Finney was photographed participating in a 10-mile race. 3. Len Sassaman Sassaman was a member of Cypherpunk and an expert in anonymous communications. He committed suicide in 2011 (close to the time of Satoshi Nakamoto's disappearance). He specialized in anonymous networks and private communications, highly aligned with the principles of Bitcoin. However, there is no direct technical or communication evidence to prove he was Satoshi Nakamoto. 4. Peter Todd HBO believes Peter Todd is Satoshi Nakamoto because in a 2010 post on the Bitcointalk forum, Peter Todd corrected a technical error made by Satoshi Nakamoto. The film speculates that Peter Todd's post was actually Satoshi Nakamoto adding his own thoughts. This requires us to believe that Satoshi Nakamoto, a master of internet security, made a most basic mistake: accidentally logging in with his real name. Furthermore, Peter Todd was only 23 years old when the Bitcoin white paper was published, which is very young for solving a problem that has troubled many veteran cryptographers. Moreover, after the documentary aired, Peter Todd showed Wired magazine photos of himself skiing or caving, and these photos were taken around the same time that Satoshi Nakamoto published his article on Bitcointalk. 5. Shinichi Mochizuki In May 2012, computer scientist Ted Nielsen believed that Satoshi Nakamoto was Japanese mathematician Shinichi Mochizuki, considering him intelligent enough that his research included the mathematical algorithms used in Bitcoin. More importantly, Mochizuki did not use conventional academic publication mechanisms, preferring to work alone. However, some have questioned this, arguing that the cryptography required to design Bitcoin was not Mochizuki's research interest. Mochizuki himself denied this. 6. Dorian Nakamoto Dorian Nakamoto is a Japanese-American engineer. Besides the shared name, Newsweek reporter Goodman found further evidence, the most compelling being that when Goodman interviewed Dorian in person and asked about Bitcoin, Dorian's response seemed to confirm his identity as the father of Bitcoin: "I'm no longer involved in it, I can't discuss it. It's been handed over to other people. They're in charge now. I have no connection with it anymore." However, in a later formal interview, Dorian denied any connection to Bitcoin, claiming he had never heard of it and that his ambiguous answer stemmed from a misunderstanding of Goodman's question: he thought Goodman was referring to his previous classified work with the military, which he had indeed undertaken. 7. Craig Steven Wright Craig Steven Wright, from Australia, is an entrepreneur and computer scientist. He was first exposed by the media in 2015, and in 2016 he publicly claimed to be Satoshi Nakamoto: claiming to possess early Bitcoin keys and provide so-called "technical proof" (his proof being Satoshi Nakamoto's cryptographic signature file, but this file has been questioned as being easily accessible to any skilled hacker on the dark web and already circulating among computer enthusiasts; another piece of evidence is the private key to the early Bitcoin blocks 1 and 9, but this private key could also have been obtained by early Bitcoin developers or their close associates). He was unable to complete public signature verification, his evidence was repeatedly questioned as forgery, and he was involved in numerous legal disputes; the mainstream community believes he is not Satoshi Nakamoto. 8. Vincent van Volkmer Since 2018, the internet has claimed that American artist Vincent van Volkmer is Satoshi Nakamoto. There is also a series of evidence supporting this claim, such as his statements that he is a mathematician and cryptographer, and that he maintains good relationships with experts who possess knowledge that hinders technological advancements. However, he himself has refuted these claims. 9. Isamu Kaneko Blockchain expert Masahiro Nakazu proposed the hypothesis that Isamu Kaneko, a Japanese programmer and developer of the file-sharing software Winny, is Satoshi Nakamoto. This hypothesis began circulating online in 2019. As evidence, Nakazu cited the relationships between theories of technical personnel who had communicated with Kaneko, the commonalities in the construction of P2P technology, and the "distrust of existing systems" revealed in his papers (Nakazu believes that Kaneko himself, whose talent and time were wasted by the state due to the Winny incident, shared the same feeling). Furthermore, Satoshi Nakamoto's never using his 1 million Bitcoins and his refusal to reveal his true identity can be explained by the fact that Kaneko is already dead. 10. Others There are also other individuals or groups who are believed to be the real Satoshi Nakamoto. These include:

  • Finnish economic sociologist Dr. Vili Lehdonvirta and Irish cryptography graduate student Michael Clear. Both denied it.

  • German and American researchers Neal King, Vladimir Oksman, and Charles Bry. They jointly applied for a patent related to Bitcoin, and the domain name bitcoin.org for the official Bitcoin project website was registered just three days after the patent application was filed. All three denied this speculation.

  • Bitcoin Foundation Chief Scientist Gavin Andresen, Mt. Gox founder Jed McCaleb, or a government agency.

  • American entrepreneur and security researcher Dustin D. Trammell, but he publicly denied it.

    • Some believe that Satoshi Nakamoto's name is actually a combination of four company names: Samsung, Toshiba, Nakamichi, and Motorola, suggesting that Bitcoin was developed jointly by these four companies and released under the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto. Conclusion The mystery of Satoshi Nakamoto's identity may not be a problem that can be solved through reasoning. In the world of cryptography, identity does not depend on narrative, but on keys. Before the early Bitcoin addresses were accessible, all discussions about "who Satoshi Nakamoto is" remained speculative. In contrast, the continued operation of Bitcoin itself is perhaps the most successful proof of this anonymous design.

data.title: South Korea Opens the Door to Legal Digital Assets, but Interest Bans and Interoperability Rules Signal Tighter Control
data.description:
Stablecoins recast as regulated cross-border money

South Korea’s ruling Democratic Party is advancing a draft Digital Asset Basic Act that reframes stablecoins as foreign exchange payment instruments, effectively treating them as digital extensions of cross-border currency flows rather than standalone crypto assets.

UPDATE: SOUTH KOREA JUST MADE RWAs AND STABLECOINS LEGAL

This is bigger than most people realize:

- Real-world assets can now be tokenized under existing laws
- Stablecoins get regulatory clarity for everyday payments
- All backed by real assets in regulated trusts

The RWA… pic.twitter.com/dGQnySeeLS

— Cryptopolitan (@CPOfficialtx) April 8, 2026

By placing these instruments under the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act, the proposal brings issuers and service providers into the scope of financial supervision without requiring an entirely new licensing regime.

This reclassification marks a decisive policy direction. Stablecoins, particularly those used in international transactions, would be monitored similarly to traditional remittance channels, giving regulators visibility into capital movement and potential risks to currency stability.

While certain everyday payments may be exempt from reporting requirements within defined thresholds, the broader framework introduces a level of scrutiny that contrasts sharply with the sector’s historically open architecture.

🇰🇷🚀 South Korea is going all-in on digital assets!

The government plans to pass the Digital Asset Second-Phase Act this year, bringing stablecoin rules, spot crypto ETFs, and clear licensing frameworks 📜

Even bigger, 25% of treasury funds could move into digital currencies by… pic.twitter.com/RE0ASh8Bpr

— SWFT Blockchain (@SwftCoin) January 9, 2026

A key provision reinforcing this stance is the reported blanket ban on interest payments for stablecoin holders. By eliminating yield-bearing incentives, the draft aims to prevent stablecoins from competing directly with bank deposits, signaling a clear intent to position them as transactional tools rather than investment products.

Tokenized assets anchored in traditional finance

Alongside stablecoins, the draft introduces strict requirements for tokenized RWAs, mandating that underlying assets be held in managed trusts under existing capital markets law. This effectively ties blockchain-based asset issuance to established custody frameworks, ensuring that on-chain representations are backed by verifiable and legally protected collateral.

금, 은, 주식 그리고 더 많은 자산까지

3월, Orderly를 통해 처리된 RWA 거래량이 사상 최고치를 기록했습니다.

온체인 RWA 총 거래량은 78% 증가했습니다.

이제 Orderly를 활용하면 단 100달러로 RWA를 지원하는 자체 perp DEX를 직접 출시할 수 있습니다.

🔗https://t.co/x7K9exg81ipic.twitter.com/UirQhoTUxG

— Orderly Korea (@OrderlyKorea) April 7, 2026

The move addresses one of the most persistent concerns surrounding tokenization: the gap between digital ownership claims and real-world enforceability. By requiring third-party trust structures, the proposal enhances investor protection and aligns tokenized assets with institutional standards, potentially making the sector more attractive to traditional financial players.

At the same time, the draft calls for the establishment of technical standards to ensure interoperability across blockchain networks. This provision highlights a forward-looking approach, acknowledging that fragmented infrastructure remains a barrier to scaling tokenized finance across multiple chains.

A compliance-first vision for digital assets

The draft reflects growing caution among policymakers, echoing earlier warnings from the Bank of Korea over the impact of stablecoins on capital flows and foreign exchange stability.

Rather than introducing a bespoke regulatory regime for digital assets, South Korea is opting to integrate them into existing legal structures, effectively absorbing Web3 innovation into the traditional financial system.

🚨 FINANCE: BANK OF KOREA AND BANK OF FRANCE HOLD JOINT SEMINAR ON DIGITAL ASSETS

The Bank of Korea and Bank of France held a two-day seminar this week covering digital assets, CBDCs, payment systems, and macroeconomic risks tied to digital finance. The event is part of a… pic.twitter.com/hAnow58qHD

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 7, 2026

This approach carries significant implications for the future of digital assets in the country. On one hand, it provides regulatory clarity and a pathway for institutional participation, particularly in areas like tokenized securities and cross-border payments. Yet, it comes with constraints that could limit some of the defining features of crypto, including permissionless yield generation and regulatory arbitrage.

By prioritizing oversight, custody, and interoperability, South Korea is positioning itself as a compliance-driven hub for digital asset innovation. The proposed framework suggests a future where blockchain-based finance operates not outside the system, but firmly within it—reshaped to meet the standards of traditional financial infrastructure while retaining the efficiency gains of tokenization.

data.title: Anthropic attempts to completely destroy Harness Engineering.
data.description:
Anthropic launched a new product today.

The positioning is: You don't need to do Harness anymore, I'll do it for you. I understand the limitations of the model best, I'll design Harness according to the model's characteristics, and package it up and sell it to you. And this package is Managed Agents.

In conclusion

Previously, everyone was saying: Harness means that the truly scarce capabilities are not inside the model, but outside the model.

But today, Anthropic is trying to put this "outside the model" layer back "inside the model manufacturer".

Harness Engineering won't die. But the window of opportunity for "human-made harnesses" may be shorter than anyone anticipates. Your Harness will either be rendered obsolete by model advancements, replaced by platform services, or you'll have to outpace both. However, realistically, general-purpose Harnesses can cover most scenarios, but not all. Legal, financial, and medical agents each have their own evaluation standards and security boundaries. Achieving 80% on a general-purpose platform isn't difficult; the final 20% is where competitiveness lies. Moreover, the open-source community will explore many more interesting avenues, such as Hermes, which I shared yesterday. Let the Agent write its own Skill, use the Skill, and modify the Skill. Don't hand over Harness to the platform; let the Agent write its own Harness.

data.title: Layer-1 Public Chain Ecosystem: Specialized Division of Labor in Blockchain Space
data.description:
Author: Tanay Ved, Senior Researcher at Coin Metrics; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • With the expansion of block space and the decrease in transaction costs, the differentiation between public chains is shifting from cost competition to scenario-based professional specialization.

  • The 20 millionth Bitcoin token was mined in March. Meanwhile, the growing ecosystem of wrapped tokens and zero-knowledge (ZK) Rollup technology is gradually unlocking Bitcoin's programmability and practical value.

  • The 20 millionth Bitcoin token was mined in March. At the same time, the growing ecosystem of wrapped tokens and zero-knowledge (ZK) Rollup technology is gradually releasing Bitcoin's programmability and practical value.

    • Ethereum is consolidating its position as an on-chain liquidity and settlement hub. As the Layer-2 network (L2) evolves from a scaling solution into a specialized execution environment, Ethereum Layer-1 (L1) fees have fallen to historic lows. Solana, through increased adoption in payment scenarios and the development of mature on-chain transaction infrastructure, is advancing its vision of an "Internet capital market." Its Alpenglow upgrade aims to achieve sub-second transaction final confirmation. Introduction With the continuous expansion of block space across networks, on-chain transaction costs have decreased significantly. Ethereum mainnet fees have fallen significantly after the recent upgrade, and Solana's single transaction cost is only a few cents. The Layer-2 network also provides a similarly low-cost execution environment. As transaction costs converge, the differentiation within the blockchain space increasingly depends on ecosystem liquidity, processing performance, and scenario specialization, rather than marginal cost advantages. In this article, we will explore how mainstream Layer-1 public chains are evolving towards differentiated positioning: Bitcoin is expanding its programmability and practical value, Ethereum is consolidating its position as a settlement and liquidity center for stablecoins, real-world assets (RWA), and decentralized finance (DeFi), while Solana focuses on high-frequency payment and trading scenarios. In March 2026, the 20 millionth Bitcoin was mined, leaving only 1 million BTC to be issued. Over 95% of the total Bitcoin supply is already in circulation, and after the halving in April 2024, the block reward decreased to 3.125 BTC, with the token issuance continuing to decrease according to the established mechanism.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    As block rewards continue to decrease, transaction fees are becoming an increasingly crucial component of miners' revenue. Aside from occasional peaks, transaction fees currently account for less than 1% of miners' total revenue. Since Bitcoin transaction fees belong entirely to miners, a long-term problem facing the Bitcoin security model is whether naturally generated transaction fee demand can sustainably fill the gap left by decreasing supply. Despite Bitcoin's market capitalization reaching approximately $1.3 trillion, about 60% of BTC has not been transferred in the past year; approximately 2.4 million BTC (about 11% of the total supply) are held on centralized exchanges, and another approximately 243,000 BTC circulate on other public chains as packaged tokens. The majority of Bitcoin's funds remain in an inactive economic state, with most transaction activity and fee revenue occurring outside the main chain. Bitcoin's functional positioning is evolving along two main lines: first, expanding the underlying programmability; and second, enhancing the productive and practical value of BTC. The growing ecosystem of sidechains, Lightning Network and other L2 solutions, Bitcoin token wrapping and liquidity staking protocols has significantly improved the utility of Bitcoin. However, these solutions also come with varying degrees of trust assumptions—ranging from fully custodial models to smart contract-based models.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    At the least trust end is Citrea, a zero-knowledge rollup (ZK-Rollup) that settles directly on the Bitcoin L1 mainchain. Citrea leverages the BitVM framework (which supports verification within Bitcoin's existing scripting system) to achieve compatibility with the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) and is secured by Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work (PoW) mechanism. ...>

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    The number of daily active addresses and transaction volume on the Ethereum mainnet has roughly doubled year-over-year, exceeding 1 million and 2.4 million respectively. However, as we previously discovered, part of this growth stems from address poisoning attacks and an increase in addresses with no economic activity (wallet transactions below $1), which sometimes constitute a significant proportion of daily active addresses.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    The Shift in the Relationship Between L1 and L2

    As transaction costs on the L1 network have significantly decreased, the role of the Ethereum L2 network is being redefined. The L2 network was initially designed as Ethereum's core scaling solution, reducing transaction costs by stripping execution layer tasks. Now, this positioning is changing. According to a recent blog post by the Ethereum Foundation, the core mission of the L2 network has shifted to providing differentiated functionality, customized services, and a specialized execution environment, while additional scaling capabilities have become a secondary value. Blob storage space (the dedicated data availability layer used by the L2 network to submit transaction data to Ethereum) is currently underutilized at less than 30%; even after recent capacity expansions, the average number of blobs per block is only about 3. Blob utilization is concentrated in a few L2 projects, and blob fees account for a negligible percentage of overall transaction fee revenue. The scaling speed of the L1 network has outpaced the settlement needs of L2, and for most L2 projects, settlement costs on Ethereum no longer constitute a substantial competitive advantage.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    The L2 projects that truly gain sustainable development momentum are those with more clearly defined and unique value. Base has established a distribution advantage by leveraging Coinbase's consumer-grade products, while Arbitrum has gained a foothold thanks to its deep DeFi liquidity. New-generation specialized public chains such as MegaETH, Lighter, Robinhood Chain, and Ink are focusing on specific application scenarios or launching innovative business models and distribution methods.

    ... The Ethereum roadmap will further promote deep integration of L1 and L2 through interoperability and a trust-least architecture, such as native Rollups, which will solidify ETH's position as the core of ecosystem liquidity and settlement. The Glamsterdam hard fork, planned for the first half of 2026, will continue this development direction. This upgrade will increase the gas limit to 200 million and introduce a parallel transaction execution mechanism, aiming to significantly improve L1 network processing capacity while reducing fees for complex smart contract interactions. The built-in proposer-builder separation mechanism (ePBS) integrates block building into the protocol's underlying layer, reducing centralization issues associated with Maximum Extractable Value (MEV) and improving transaction ordering transparency. These changes aim to make Ethereum L1 a more competitive execution environment, ensuring the underlying network remains the most reliable platform for high-value settlement and decentralized finance. Solana is shedding its early label as a "retail investor and Meme coin public chain" and moving towards its vision of building an "internet capital market." With transaction fees below 1 cent and block times within 400 milliseconds, Solana is a natural platform for applications with extremely high speed requirements, such as payments, micro-payments, and high-frequency trading. This characteristic is attracting a number of specialized applications that require large-scale, low-latency execution.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    Payments and High-Frequency Small-Amount Payments

    Solana's low-cost network environment makes it a leading public chain in the payment and consumer-grade value transfer field. Daily USDC transfers under $1000 remain stable at approximately 3 million transactions, with the median transaction size consistently below $100.

    Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    Trading Infrastructure

    Solana's processing power has also attracted professional-grade on-chain trading infrastructure to set up operations there. Proprietary Automated Market Makers (propAMMs) built by professional trading institutions using non-public off-chain pricing models operate more like dark pools than ordinary decentralized exchanges (DEXs). The pricing curves of automated market makers (AMMs) like Uniswap are vulnerable to front-running and arbitrage attacks. In contrast, proprietary market makers update prices off-chain and settle on Solana, making them resistant to MEV (Mean Ever-Earned Transaction) extraction. Alpenglow and Other Upgrades Upcoming infrastructure upgrades will further strengthen Solana's advantages in these areas. The Alpenglow upgrade will replace the existing Tower BFT and Proof-of-History (PoH) mechanism with the lightweight voting aggregation protocol Votor, aiming to reduce block final confirmation time from approximately 12 seconds to 100–150 milliseconds. The Block Assembly Market (BAM) developed by Jito allows trading applications to control transaction ordering, supports features such as priority cancellation, and improves execution fairness. Conclusion As blockchain space expands and transaction costs decrease, the core competition among public blockchain networks is shifting from cost to specialized division of labor. Mainstream L1 public blockchains are leveraging their architectural advantages to meet diverse scenario needs; meanwhile, public blockchains focused on specific applications are optimizing around niche requirements—such as Hyperliquid focusing on transactions, Canton for institutional finance, and Arc and Tempo serving enterprise-level stablecoin payments—making clear trade-offs in access permissions, compliance, and execution design. The core question for the future is: how will the industry landscape evolve as on-chain demand grows at scale? The on-chain infrastructure industry still faces a common risk: a white paper released by Google's Quantum AI team on March 31st indicated that the number of physical qubits required to crack the elliptic curve cryptography algorithm that secures Bitcoin, Ethereum, and most blockchains may be less than 500,000, only about 1/20th of the previous estimate of 20 million. Early solutions such as Bitcoin's BIP-360 proposal and Ethereum's post-quantum cryptography roadmap have gradually taken shape. However, a deeper challenge lies in how to coordinate community consensus and promote voluntary adoption in decentralized networks, a process that is often slower and more uncertain than that of centralized institutions.

data.title: Iran's encrypted tollbooth in the Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
Source: TRM Team; Translation: Jinse Finance Claw

Key Points of this Article：

  • Iran has begun accepting cryptocurrency as a payment option for transit fees in the Strait of Hormuz since mid-March 2026, marking the first time a country has used cryptocurrency infrastructure as a sovereign revenue mechanism in a major maritime chokepoint.

  • The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) charges up to $2 million per vessel; different public estimates suggest that the charging system could generate up to $20 million in revenue daily from oil tankers alone, and potentially $600 million to $800 million per month if liquefied natural gas carriers are included.

  • The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) charges up to $2 million per vessel; different public estimates suggest that the charging system could generate up to $20 million per day from oil tankers alone, and potentially $600 million to $800 million per month if liquefied natural gas carriers are included.

  • The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) charges up to $2 million per vessel; different public estimates suggest that the charging system could generate up to $20 million per day from oil tankers alone, and potentially $600 million to $800 million per month if liquefied natural gas carriers are included.

    • The anonymous intermediary responsible for managing the fees has not yet disclosed its identity, creating a critical information gap for any future enforcement or sanctions actions against the payment network. Cryptocurrency payments can be settled quickly and do not go through the US correspondent banking system, making real-time interception of transit fee payments extremely difficult technically. The Pakistan-brokered ceasefire agreement took effect on April 7, 2026, but according to a statement from the Iranian government on April 8, the status of the agreement may change. The transit fee collection remains in effect, and Iran has established institutional infrastructure (specific regulations, the command structure of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, and the Qeshm Island cryptocurrency exchange channel), indicating that the mechanism is unlikely to be withdrawn in the short term. Iran's use of digital assets as a means of payment for transit fees through the Strait of Hormuz is a key deployment of cryptocurrency at the national level to circumvent sanctions. Since mid-March 2026, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has been charging ship operators passing through the strait up to $2 million per vessel, accepting payments in RMB (via Kunlun Bank's Cross-Border Interbank Payment System (CIPS) to bypass SWIFT), Bitcoin, or possibly USDT. Iranian parliamentary legislation stipulates that the fees are denominated in rials and authorizes payments using "digital currencies" involving Iranian companies. Iran approved the "Strait of Hormuz Management Plan" on March 30-31, 2026, formalizing an already operational system. Based on current traffic volume, public analysis suggests that the toll system could generate up to $20 million daily from oil tankers alone; if LNG carriers are included, monthly revenue could reach $600 million to $800 million.

    1. How does the Strait of Hormuz toll system work?

    Although vessels began transiting the strait on April 8, it remains unclear whether the ceasefire agreement reached last night will affect the toll system in the short term. The typical operation process of this toll system is as follows: Contact and Declaration: Ship operators contact anonymous intermediary companies affiliated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and submit ship ownership certificates, flag registration information, cargo manifests, port of destination, crew list, and AIS tracking data; Security Review: The IRGC screens ships—verifying their connections to the United States or Israel; ships from hostile countries (such as the United States and Israel) are completely prohibited from passage; Nationality Classification: Iran uses a five-tier nationality rating system—classifying countries into levels 1-5 based on "friendliness," with higher friendliness levels resulting in lower applicable rates; **Fee Negotiation:** Tanker fees are based on approximately US$0.5 to US$1 per barrel of crude oil (rates vary by country class); a fully loaded Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) (approximately 2 million barrels) will cost approximately US$2 million; fees for container ships and other vessel types will be negotiated separately. **Payment Method:** Only RMB (via Kunlun Bank/CIPS system) or digital assets are accepted. **Passage Permit:** Upon payment, the vessel will receive a VHF radio pass code and be escorted by the IRGC Navy through the channel north of Larak Island. **2. Why does the Iranian regime accept cryptocurrency?** Iran's choice to accept cryptocurrency as a payment method for passage fees through the Strait of Hormuz is not a departure from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps' (IRGC) existing financial strategy, but rather an extension of it. As TRM Labs documented before the U.S. Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) imposed sanctions on Zedcex and Zedxion in January 2026, the IRGC had already transferred approximately $1 billion through offshore, exchange-branded stablecoin infrastructure. This leverages the same characteristics that attract legitimate users: low transaction costs, high throughput, deep broker liquidity, and widespread regional adoption—all while completely bypassing the U.S. correspondent banking system. The Hormuz fee system deploys the same architecture, transforming an existing sanction circumvention channel into a real-time revenue collection mechanism for the Iranian state. Cryptocurrency transactions can be settled quickly and without going through the U.S. correspondent banking system, making them difficult to freeze or intercept in real time. Which cryptocurrencies does Iran accept? According to Iranian state media, the Iranian parliament has passed legislation listing the rial, yuan, and digital currency as payment methods, but the full text of the bill has not been made public, making it impossible to confirm the specific currency from the legal text itself. Iranian state media and some officials specifically mentioned Bitcoin, while Western media, citing shipping industry sources, reported that the cryptocurrency collected by the IRGC in practice is USDT. Although the use of USDT aligns with the IRGC's documented pattern of behavior, the IRGC may be more inclined to believe that Bitcoin has specific operational considerations: centralized stablecoin issuers can freeze funds and cooperate with law enforcement to confiscate illicit proceeds. 3. How Iran Explains the Role of Cryptocurrency in the Hormuz Fee System Iranian officials and their affiliated media, when describing this fee system, primarily define it as a declaration of sovereign rights, rather than a wartime revenue-collecting measure. Parliamentary supporters of the Hormuz management plan claim that the strait is "no different from other transit routes—we guarantee its security, and it is only natural that ships and tankers pay us." A media outlet affiliated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) described the system as "a tollbooth Iran has set up at the throat of world trade," calling it a "long-overdue exercise of sovereignty." Iran legally defends its toll collection, pointing out that it has never ratified the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea—which explicitly prohibits toll collection in international straits—and therefore, within Iran's narrative, it has the right to charge for the "services" it provides, including security escorts, navigation guidance, and environmental monitoring.

    Hamid Hosseini, spokesman for the Iranian Oil, Gas and Petrochemical Exporters Union, told Iranian media that the toll system helps "monitor goods entering and leaving the strait and ensure that the waterway is not used for weapons transport"—characterizing cryptocurrency tolls as a censorship and monitoring mechanism, rather than just a financial tool. Iranian customs has set up a dedicated digital currency exchange window on Qeshm Island to quickly convert digital assets into rials or transfer them to overseas accounts after collection—indicating that Iran is consciously building infrastructure for large-scale cryptocurrency processing.

    ... An Iranian financial media outlet published an analysis stating that the toll system, operating at full capacity, could generate $120 billion in annual revenue, an order of magnitude higher than the $600-800 million per month estimate in Western reports. It has been described as Iran's "last" bargaining chip in any peace negotiations. Iran has characterized the toll system as a permanent sovereign claim—established through legislation, managed by a permanent Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps command structure, and supported by a dedicated cryptocurrency exchange infrastructure on Qeshm Island—making a complete dismantling of the system after a conflict unlikely. The cryptocurrency dimension exacerbates enforcement challenges: the characteristics of rapid settlement and independence from the US correspondent banking system will not change with a ceasefire. The US Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control's ability to sanction this payment infrastructure depends on identifying and locking down the currently anonymous toll-collecting intermediary before funds are dispersed, an institution whose identity has yet to be publicly revealed. If this toll system continues in the long term, it will mark the first time that stablecoin infrastructure has been established as a national-level, sustainable fiscal revenue tool in a major global maritime chokepoint. 5. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) Does Iran accept cryptocurrency as a toll for passage through the Strait of Hormuz? Yes. Since mid-March 2026, the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has accepted cryptocurrency as a payment method for passage fees through the Strait of Hormuz. Ships can also use RMB to pay through Kunlun Bank via the Cross-border RMB Payment System (CIPS). The Strait of Hormuz Management Plan, approved by the Iranian Parliament on March 30-31, 2026, formally codifies this system. What is the cost of passing through the Strait of Hormuz? The fees are based on vessel type and country rating. Tanker fees start at approximately $0.5 to $1 per barrel of crude oil, meaning a Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) carrying approximately 2 million barrels of crude oil would pay around $2 million. Container ship fees are negotiated separately. Iran uses a five-tier country rating system; countries considered "more friendly" receive lower rates, while vessels linked to the US and Israel are completely barred from passage. How does the Strait of Hormuz toll system work? Ship operators contact an anonymous intermediary company linked to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and submit vessel ownership records, flag registration information, cargo manifests, port of destination, crew lists, and AIS tracking data. The IRGC then screens each vessel for links to the US and Israel. Vessels granted passage agree on a fee (paid in RMB or cryptocurrency) through negotiation and receive a VHF broadcast passage code, then pass through the channel north of Larak Island under the escort of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy. How much revenue can Iran obtain from the Strait of Hormuz passage fees? TRM estimates that the system could generate up to $20 million daily from oil tankers alone; if LNG carriers are included, monthly revenue could reach $600 million to $800 million. Iranian economic analysts estimate that annual revenue could reach $120 billion at full capacity, but this figure assumes a fuller recovery of passage and coverage of all vessel types. Can the US Treasury or the US government block Iran's cryptocurrency fees? Real-time interception is technically very difficult—transactions are settled quickly and typically do not pass through the US banking system. The identities of the anonymous intermediaries currently responsible for collecting fees are not publicly disclosed, which limits the ability to take direct action against the payment network. Stablecoin issuers can blacklist relevant addresses, but this first requires identifying these addresses.

data.title: Hormuz Goes Crypto: Iran’s Bitcoin Toll Strategy Could Mint Millions Daily
data.description:
Iran is moving swiftly to assert control over the Strait of Hormuz, unveiling a Bitcoin-based toll system for oil tankers just days after agreeing to a temporary ceasefire with the United States—an audacious move that blends geopolitics with crypto rails.

🚨 JUST IN: There have been NO new missiles fired into Israel tonight as the ceasefire with Iran continues

Good! Keep it up!

JD VANCE is set to lead a delegation in Pakistan this weekend with Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner 🇺🇸 pic.twitter.com/syv6gmeHhU

— Eric Daugherty (@EricLDaugh) April 9, 2026

With more than a third of the world's crude oil supply having to pass through the narrow waterway every year, this idea could potentially turn the straits into a golden goose for Iran.

Bitcoin Turns Hormuz Straits Into Its Jackpot

In the immediate aftermath of the ceasefire, Iranian authorities announced that all oil tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz must pay a $1-per-barrel fee in Bitcoin. The proposal effectively transforms one of the world’s most critical energy corridors into a state-controlled, crypto-powered toll gate.

🚨 BITCOIN TOLL: IRAN’S CRYPTO MOVE AT HORMUZ!

Iran has imposed a tax of $1/barrel on tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz, for which payment will be made exclusively in Bitcoin.

According to the Financial Times, Iran is taking this step to evade US sanctions and to… pic.twitter.com/DuMyUqP5eD

— Crypto Aman (@cryptoamanclub) April 8, 2026

Ships passing by the straits will be required to submit cargo details in advance and wait for approval from Iranian authorities. Once cleared, operators will have to immediately complete a Bitcoin payment to a government-controlled wallet before being allowed to pass.

Iranian officials argue that using Bitcoin ensures the toll system cannot be traced, blocked, or confiscated under international sanctions, giving Tehran a financial workaround at a time when traditional channels remain restricted.

Iran’s unilateral move is unlikely to sit well with other major oil exporters in the region, including Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar, all of whom depend on the Strait of Hormuz for energy exports.

🚨BREAKING: Countries that can now pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Malaysia. Thailand. South Africa. Philippines. India. Pakistan. Sri Lanka. Bangladesh. China.

The US is losing control. Nations that refused to bow are moving freely. The empire can threaten. It cannot stop… pic.twitter.com/AkCmja2hWS

— New Direction AFRICA (@Its_ereko) April 2, 2026

The decision to impose Bitcoin-based tolls—without broader coordination—signals Tehran’s intent to convert military leverage into economic control, even during a fragile pause in hostilities.

With oil markets already sensitive to disruptions, the introduction of a crypto toll layer adds a new and unpredictable variable to global energy logistics.

U.S. Wants A Slice Of The Golden Goose

Adding another twist, Donald Trump suggested that the United States and Iran could potentially operate a joint toll system for the strait, describing the idea as “a beautiful thing.”

🚨BREAKING: Countries that can now pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Malaysia. Thailand. South Africa. Philippines. India. Pakistan. Sri Lanka. Bangladesh. China.

The US is losing control. Nations that refused to bow are moving freely. The empire can threaten. It cannot stop… pic.twitter.com/AkCmja2hWS

— New Direction AFRICA (@Its_ereko) April 2, 2026

But that vision appears far from reality. Iranian officials have made no indication of sharing control, and their Bitcoin-based framework points in the opposite direction—toward sovereign, sanctions-resistant control of the waterway.

For now, the message is clear: while Washington may want a stake in the world’s most important oil corridor, Tehran is building a system designed to operate without it.

data.title: The New York Times is wrong. Satoshi Nakamoto is not Adam Back, but Finney.
data.description:
It took a year to use stylistic analysis to point to a living person, then ignored the dead, undeniable person. This wasn't an investigation; it was choosing a suspect who could be interviewed.

It took a year to use stylistic analysis to point to a living person, then ignored the dead, undeniable person.

...

Alan Walker·April 8, 2026·Zombie Café

This morningSilicon Valley Alan WalkerSitting in Zombie Café, I saw a tweet from The New York Times:

Adam Back, El Salvador, a 55-year-old cryptographer, is none other than the legendary Satoshi Nakamoto. He took a sip of coffee. John Carreyou, a reporter for *The New York Times*, did something clever in this report: he chose a living person. Adam Back could be flown in for an interview, filmed in a hotel room, his "nervous body language" recorded. He could deny it, and his denial itself would become part of the news. That's good journalism, but not good investigation. Alan, who has been in this industry for thirty years, has seen far too many cases of packaging the "most convenient answer" as the "most reasonable answer." Satoshi Nakamoto's true identity has never been about who is alive and can be interviewed. It's about the chain of evidence. The chain of evidence points to Hal Finney. Let's first discuss the loopholes in the NYT case. The core evidence presented by New York Times reporter Carreyrou is: Stylistic analysis shows that Adam Back's writing style is most similar to Satoshi Nakamoto's; Back's "mysterious silence" on cryptography mailing lists between 2008 and 2010; Back's invention of Hashcash, the precursor to Bitcoin's proof-of-work; and Back's "nervousness" in an HBO documentary.

A more crucial flaw: Back submitted five emails between himself and Satoshi Nakamoto as evidence in the Craig Wright case. These emails show that the two are different people. Carreyrou's rebuttal is: "Back may have sent these emails to himself as cover."

This statement appeared in a New York Times investigative report. He has no evidence to support this speculation.

I am not saying that Adam Back is not Satoshi Nakamoto.

I am not saying that Adam Back is not Satoshi Nakamoto.

... I'm saying: if your strongest evidence requires the assumption that "he may have forged the emails he submitted to the court," your case is invalid. Now let's talk about why Finney's case is stronger. Hal Finney died in August 2014 from complications of ALS at the age of 58. He was diagnosed in August 2009 and gradually lost his ability to type. He denied being Satoshi Nakamoto until his death. He was unable to defend himself. This was his biggest weakness as a suspect and the biggest reason everyone ignored him. But let's look at the chain of evidence. The First Clue: The First Bitcoin Transaction box-sizing:="" color:="" font-weight:="" line-height:="" span="" text="" carreyrou="" p="" adam="" back="" hbo="" nodeleaf="" img="" src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/7449672_image3.png" type="block" craig="" wright="" strong="" hal="" finney="" als="" 0px="">

The recipient of the most important first transaction in history is someone you trust. Or, it's yourself. ...

Second Clue: He lives next to "Satoshi Nakamoto"

The third clue: Code Gene

Finney created it in 2004

Sixth Clue: He Cares But Never Shows Off

Finney's BitcoinTalk published in 2013 In his post, "Bitcoin and Me," he recounts in the first person how he "discovered" Bitcoin, how he became the first downloader, and how he accepted the first transaction. The article is written with deep emotion, brimming with passion for the project. A person can speak of themselves in this tone. Especially someone whose body is being slowly eroded by ALS, reflecting on the most important creation of their life. He predicted that each Bitcoin would be worth $1 million in 2011. This wasn't a prediction from an outsider. This was a prediction from someone who knew the code limit was 21 million, knew the system's design logic, and knew the system wouldn't fail. A Comparison with Adam Back: Back's silence can be explained in a hundred ways: lawyer's advice, weariness of the media, or not wanting to provide any fertile ground for a false speculation. Back's "nervousness" is a normal reaction to someone suddenly being identified as the founder of Bitcoin in a documentary. Finney's evidence is structured. It's embedded in the first transaction on the blockchain, embedded in his neighbor's name, embedded in his code, embedded in the timeline of Satoshi Nakamoto's disappearance. Which race is the counter-argument? I know this is the most frequently cited one. Finney was running, Satoshi Nakamoto was sending emails, so they're not the same person. Consider the following two possibilities. The first possibility: This does prove that Finney is not Satoshi Nakamoto, then Satoshi Nakamoto is Adam Back. The evidence is "stylistic analysis uncertainty" plus "tense body language." The second possibility: The transactions and emails were prepared in advance, or involved some degree of collaboration, and Finney was the core organizer and executor of this collaboration. Lopp himself acknowledges the existence of the second possibility. He chose to abandon it because he believes Bitcoin was created by a single person. But he also has no evidence to prove this—it's his assumption. If we allow Carreyrou to assume, without evidence, that "Back may have forged court mail," then we can also allow ourselves to assume that "Finney may have pre-arranged these transactions." The evidentiary basis for both assumptions is the same: zero. But one of them has a much stronger overall evidentiary structure. I don't need your agreement. I just want you to read this after you've read Carreyrou's report. Then you can judge for yourself whose chain of evidence is stronger. My coffee's gone cold.
